Most DTC brands don't know how to use UGC content. They keep hiring new creators, testing new hooks, and swapping out music.

But their ads are still unpalatably beige.

The problem isn't the format.

It’s the lack of signal.

You don't need more "User Generated Content."

UGC has become a commodity. Consumers in 2026 have developed a biological radar for "fake organic" videos.

Simply sharing a video of a person isn't enough. You have to stop "Persuading" and start "Reassuring."

The secret is in the hidden vocabulary in your reviews.

The Meta/Google algorithms no longer care about the interest boxes you check. The Creative is the Targeting.

If you use "Brand-Speak," you get broad, expensive traffic.

If you use the specific "Human-Speak" from your reviews, you target via psychology.

People don't buy the "best" product.

They buy the one they won't regret.

Traditional UGC sells the "Dream."

Review-derived storytelling sells the "Reality."

By highlighting specific frictions and outcomes from real customers, you remove the emotional risk of the purchase.

Anyone can generate 1,000 AI-scripts.

What is rare and "expensive" is Listening.

When your ads use the messy, irregular truth found in a 3-paragraph review, that effort creates an "Attention Debt" that the consumer pays with their time.

---

I help established DTC brands stop the "Guesswork Tax" by marketing the contrarian way.

Instead of letting an agency guess what makes your product interesting, we use the R2R (Review To Revenue) model to build assets that your audience actually recognises themselves in.

The Micro-Audit is a low-risk way to replace generic, low-performing copy with language that actually moves the needle.

Micro-Persona Profile: A breakdown of emotional triggers.

3 High-Intent Ads: Copy-paste hooks extracted directly from the "Voice of the Customer."

Is This For You?

We only work with established brands to ensure the depth of data required for success:

Minimum 2 years in operation.

At least 100 customer reviews available.

Focus on one specific product for maximum impact.

Investment: £400 (Fixed Fee). No long-term contracts.

The Offer: I am giving a 30% discount to the next 3 brands to book a Micro-Audit in exchange for a video testimonial.

How to Start: DM me "R2R" here. I’ll send a 1-minute questionnaire to get started.

We are Hiring! Personal Banker / Financial Solutions Advisor

Job Description
Job Title: Personal Banker / Financial Solutions Advisor

Job Type: Part-time, Contract

Location: Remote

Job Summary:
Join our customer’s team as a Personal Banker / Financial Solutions Advisor and use your front-line service experience to help design the next generation of everyday personal finance planning. You’ll work asynchronously with a leading AI lab, reviewing realistic client scenarios and providing detailed feedback so that AI systems can better support financial services professionals and average households with guidance on day-to-day banking, budgeting, debt management, and basic investing decisions.

Key Responsibilities:
• Review scenarios that mirror real branch conversations (expense budgeting, mortgage and retirement planning, building savings, investing, etc.) for a wide range of clients and describe how you would advise them.
• Evaluate AI-generated guidance for suitability, clarity, and practicality for typical mass-market and mid-market customers.
• Document the questions, conversation flow, and decision rules you use in-branch (e.g., when to recommend specific solutions vs. refer to a specialist).
• Provide written feedback on how to improve explanations, next steps, and tradeoff framing for non-expert clients.
• Highlight compliance and risk considerations and clearly define when you would avoid giving advice or escalate to another team.

Required Skills and Qualifications:
• Several years of hands-on experience in roles such as Personal Banker, Relationship Banker, Universal/Branch Banker, or Financial Solutions Advisor at a major bank or credit union (e.g., Bank of America / Merrill, Chase, Wells Fargo, Citi, PNC, Truist, regional banks).
• Proven track record conducting needs-based financial conversations and matching everyday clients to appropriate banking and basic investment solutions.
• Strong familiarity with core consumer products: checking and savings accounts, CDs, debit/credit cards, overdraft policies, personal/auto loans, basic retirement/investment accounts, and digital banking tools.
• Experience helping clients with budgeting, expense management, debt payoff strategies, and building emergency savings.
• Strong personal financial planning experience for a wide range of clients.
• Exceptional written communication skills, with the ability to explain financial concepts in clear, empathetic, plain language.
• High integrity, strong compliance mindset, and strict commitment to client confidentiality.

Send us your Resume!
Violeta Zelaya

Job Description
Job Title: Financial Advisor (CFA or CFP) 

Job Type: Consulting/Contract

Location: Remote

Job Summary:
Join our customer’s team as an Everyday Personal Finance Advisor (CFP®) AI Trainer and help shape the future of AI-assisted financial planning. In this impactful role, you’ll leverage your expertise with mainstream American households to review real-world client scenarios, evaluate AI-generated guidance, and offer actionable feedback to ensure technology truly serves both advisors and clients. You’ll collaborate asynchronously with a leading AI lab, focusing on practical, empathetic advice for everyday people navigating budgeting, debt, saving, retirement planning, and basic investing decisions.

Key Responsibilities:
Evaluate and review realistic advisory scenarios involving budgets, emergency funds, debt management, retirement planning, and investment decisions tailored to mainstream households.
Assess AI-generated recommendations for suitability, clarity, compliance, and actionable practicality for W-2 earners and middle-income families.
Document how actual advisor-client conversations unfold, including prioritizing goals, discussing tradeoffs, and walking clients through decision points.
Detail decision rules, rules of thumb, and benchmarks commonly used in practice (e.g., cash reserve targets, debt payoff sequencing, retirement savings rates).
Deliver clear, plain-language written feedback to enhance AI explanations, next steps, and client communication.
Identify and flag compliance or risk issues, emphasizing a fiduciary, client-first approach and appropriate referral scenarios (CPA, attorney, insurance).
Contribute to the development of workflows and processes that reflect best practices in everyday personal finance advising.

Required Skills and Qualifications:
Several years of hands-on, client-facing experience providing holistic financial planning to mainstream households in roles such as Financial Advisor, Wealth Advisor, or Personal Financial Consultant.
Expertise in budgeting, cash flow analysis, debt management, retirement savings strategies, college planning, and basic risk management (insurance needs analysis).
CFP® certification strongly preferred (or CFP exam passed with substantial relevant experience); CFA, ChFC, or similar planning credentials considered with strong mainstream client work.
Exceptional written and verbal communication skills, with a talent for breaking down complex topics for non-experts.
Demonstrated commitment to fiduciary standards, compliance, and high-integrity client service.
Experience mentoring others, developing workflows, or standardizing planning processes.
Comfort working asynchronously and independently in a remote setting.

The collapse of software layers driven by agent-based AI platforms is repositioning the operational and governance landscape for IT service providers—putting emphasis on customer outcomes, workforce skill shifts, and deeper governance requirements.

• Tectonic, led by Dusty Gulleson, is actively deploying AI agents to automate traditional help desk tasks and routine workflow interactions, citing direct cost savings such as negating $50,000 annual API license fees for clients by creating custom interoperability solutions in hours rather than weeks.

• Higher education and government sectors are formalizing policies that prohibit AI usage, with Dusty Gulleson citing strict “no AI” clauses in MSAs, underscoring persistent governance roadblocks and cultural resistance in regulated industries.

• The shift toward agentic AI is accelerating the collapse of traditional IT roles—level one and two help desk functions are now primarily focused on empathy and client relationships, while technical tasks are increasingly performed by AI agents. This repositions hiring priorities from technical competencies to business analysis and customer service skill.

For MSPs and IT leaders, these developments highlight the operational need to revisit workforce planning, contract governance, and the risk of accumulating new forms of technical debt as the underlying IT stack evolves.

In 2026, your AI assistant isn't just writing your emails—it is managing your budget and hiring its own digital team. 🤖💸

The internet was built for human eyeballs, but today, it is being rapidly rebuilt for Artificial Intelligence. We have officially entered the era of the Agentic Economy.

In this new landscape, your AI is no longer just a tool; it is a "Digital Buyer." Using Non-Human Identities (NHI) and programmable utility wallets, your AI can now identify a problem, find a specialized "freelancer" AI agent to fix it, negotiate a price, and execute a payment—all while you are asleep. 🌍🔐

In our latest AI News & Trends report on AI Buzz, we pull back the curtain on "Agent-to-Agent" (A2A) commerce and the massive structural shift it is causing in the global economy.

Inside the full report, you’ll discover: 
🤝 The Digital Handshake: How agents from different companies (like Google and Microsoft) use the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to talk to each other. 
💰 Programmable Money: How AI uses "Smart Contracts" to pay for services without a human filling out a checkout form. 
⚠️ The Autonomy Risk: Why "Agentic Spirals" can drain a corporate budget in seconds if you don't have hard budget ceilings in place.

The human role is shifting from "Worker" to "Orchestrator." Your job is no longer to do the task, but to set the goals and the guardrails for your digital workforce. 🛡️💼

The important emails were flagged. And sitting in my drafts folder were responses -- good ones -- ready for me to review and hit send.

I didn't hire a virtual assistant. I didn't stay up late. I built an AI skill in about 20 minutes before bed.

No code. No developer. No six-figure software contract. No recruiter fees. Just a conversation.

📧 Here's what happened: I told Claude to create a "skill" -- basically a set of instructions that teaches AI exactly how I work. Which emails matter to me. Which ones can wait? How I like to respond. My tone, my priorities, my "ignore this forever" list. Now it runs overnight, reads my inbox, flags what's urgent, and drafts replies in my voice, sitting in my drafts folder. I wake up, review, tweak if needed, and send.

That's not autocomplete. That's a digital team member who actually read the onboarding docs. (So already outperforming about 90% of new hires. I kid. Mostly.)

🤔 Think about that for a second. How many hours a week do you spend triaging email? Reading stuff that doesn't matter? Writing the same "Thanks for sending this over, let me loop in the team" response for the 400th time? What if that was just... done?

💡 The difference between using AI and 𝗔𝗖𝗧𝗨𝗔𝗟𝗟𝗬 using AI is this: most people type one-off prompts and hope for the best. That's like Googling "how to cook" every single night instead of just learning the recipe.

Skills let you encode your expertise, your workflows, and your standards so AI performs consistently every time. You teach it once. It remembers forever. Unlike Dave from accounting, who still can't remember which Slack channel to post in after three years.

🎯 Think of it like this:
• One-off prompts = hiring a temp who needs the full onboarding packet every Monday morning
• AI Skills = training a team member who never forgets, never calls in sick, and never microwaves fish in the break room

The companies that figure this out first won't just be more efficient. They'll be operating on a completely different level while everyone else is still copy-pasting ChatGPT outputs into Word docs and calling it "digital transformation."

🚀 So here's my challenge to you: what's the one repetitive task on your plate that you keep doing manually, that follows the same pattern every time, that makes you think "why am I still doing this in 2026?" That's your first skill.

I woke up this morning to something weird. My inbox was already sorted. The important emails were flagged. And sitting in my drafts folder were responses -- good ones -- ready for me to review and hit send.

I didn't hire a virtual assistant. I didn't stay up late. I built an AI skill in about 20 minutes before bed.

No code. No developer. No six-figure software contract. No recruiter fees. Just a conversation.

📧 Here's what happened: I told Claude to create a "skill" -- basically a set of instructions that teaches AI exactly how I work. Which emails matter to me. Which ones can wait? How I like to respond. My tone, my priorities, my "ignore this forever" list. Now it runs overnight, reads my inbox, flags what's urgent, and drafts replies in my voice, sitting in my drafts folder. I wake up, review, tweak if needed, and send.

That's not autocomplete. That's a digital team member who actually read the onboarding docs. (So already outperforming about 90% of new hires. I kid. Mostly.)

🤔 Think about that for a second. How many hours a week do you spend triaging email? Reading stuff that doesn't matter? Writing the same "Thanks for sending this over, let me loop in the team" response for the 400th time? What if that was just... done?

💡 The difference between using AI and 𝗔𝗖𝗧𝗨𝗔𝗟𝗟𝗬 using AI is this: most people type one-off prompts and hope for the best. That's like Googling "how to cook" every single night instead of just learning the recipe.

Skills let you encode your expertise, your workflows, and your standards so AI performs consistently every time. You teach it once. It remembers forever. Unlike Dave from accounting, who still can't remember which Slack channel to post in after three years.

🎯 Think of it like this:
• One-off prompts = hiring a temp who needs the full onboarding packet every Monday morning
• AI Skills = training a team member who never forgets, never calls in sick, and never microwaves fish in the break room

The companies that figure this out first won't just be more efficient. They'll be operating on a completely different level while everyone else is still copy-pasting ChatGPT outputs into Word docs and calling it "digital transformation."

🚀 So here's my challenge to you: what's the one repetitive task on your plate that you keep doing manually, that follows the same pattern every time, that makes you think "why am I still doing this in 2026?" That's your first skill.

Negotiations resume on 4/27 and according to Occupy Cinema, SAG-AFTRA is thinking of doing the following. I’m a crew member. What about the crew? Sean Astin…. I…. Have NO words: 

SAG-AFTRA wants to tax AI performers. If a studio replaces a human with AI, it would pay that performer's salary equivalent into the pension fund.
ㅤ
The SAG-AFTRA AI task force developed a studio tax proposal as one of several mechanisms for addressing AI displacement in the 2026 contract negotiations. The proposal would require productions covered by the agreement to calculate the cost of any AI-generated performance, including background performances, principal performances, and voice performances, and contribute the equivalent of that cost to the Screen Actors Guild-Producers Pension and Health Plans. The mechanism is designed to remove the primary financial incentive for AI replacement: the ability to generate a performance at a fraction of the cost of hiring a union performer while avoiding health and pension contributions.
ㅤ
The proposal has significant limitations that its architects have acknowledged. It applies only to SAG-AFTRA-covered productions, which excludes non-union productions, content produced outside the United States under foreign contracts, and AI-generated content produced by platforms that do not operate under union agreements. It also does not address the use of AI-generated background performers in crowd scenes, which has already been piloted by several major productions as a way to reduce costs without triggering principal performer contract provisions. The proposal is one mechanism among several being negotiated. Whether any form of the AI tax language survives the negotiation and reaches the final contract depends on how much financial pressure the studios are under and how willing the union's membership is to strike in support of it.
ㅤ

Some of the most forward-thinking CFOs I talk to right now are choosing contractors over permanent hires for certain roles. It's not about budget. It's about optionality.

Here's what I'm hearing from finance leaders building teams in 2026.

"We know AI and automation will change some of this work in the next 12 to 24 months. Why would we hire someone permanently into a role we know will look completely different - or may not exist - by next year?"

That's not pessimism. That's smart org design.

The pattern we're seeing across our clients is consistent. Manual, high-volume finance work - reconciliations, journal entries, data validation - is being covered by strong senior contractors. Those contractors bridge the gap while the automation layer gets built underneath. Meanwhile, permanent headcount is being invested in roles that require judgment, leadership and strategic ownership - the positions that are becoming more valuable, not less.

The result: the work gets done now without locking in fixed costs for roles that are obvious automation candidates, and the permanent hires you do make are higher impact and longer lasting.

AI is accelerating this shift faster than most people realize. Finance leaders who are paying attention are already adjusting their hiring mix.

The companies winning the talent game aren't choosing between permanent and contract. They're using both intentionally - contract for the work that's changing, permanent for the people who will lead through that change.

Is your team thinking about talent this way? Happy to share what we're seeing across the market.

Your VA costs $2,000/month.
This costs $20.

I'm not exaggerating.

Last week I sat down and mapped out the tasks that most small businesses are paying real people to do, manually, slowly, and with mistakes.

Then I built automations for each one.

Here's what I found:

𝗧𝗮𝘀𝗸 𝟭: Lead data entry
Someone fills your form → your assistant copies it into a spreadsheet → sometimes forgets → you lose the lead.
Automation: Form → Make.com → CRM. Done in 0.3 seconds. Zero errors.

𝗧𝗮𝘀𝗸 𝟮: Follow-up emails
Your team sends follow-ups "when they remember."
Automation: AI writes a personalized reply based on what the lead asked → sends within 60 seconds → you look like you have a 24/7 sales team.

𝗧𝗮𝘀𝗸 𝟯: Invoice reminders
Nobody likes chasing payments. So most people just... don't.
Automation: Stripe triggers a polite WhatsApp reminder on day 3, day 7, day 14. No awkwardness.

𝗧𝗮𝘀𝗸 𝟰: Reporting
Every Monday someone spends 3 hours screenshotting dashboards and pasting them into a Google Doc.
Automation: Make pulls the data, formats it, drops it into a live dashboard. Monday morning = done before coffee.

𝗧𝗮𝘀𝗸 𝟱: Client onboarding
You send a welcome email. Then a contract. Then a questionnaire. Then access links. 5 separate emails, half get lost.
Automation: Client fills one form → everything triggers automatically. Contract, docs, access, welcome message. All of it.

Total cost for all 5?
Roughly $20-50/month in tool subscriptions.

Total time to set up?
About 2-3 hours each.

I'm not saying fire your team.
I'm saying stop making them do work that a robot should be doing so they can focus on stuff that actually needs a human brain.

The businesses that figure this out in 2026 will run circles around the ones still hiring people to copy-paste data.

Save this if you're not ready today but know you will be.

And if you're a business owner who looked at this list and thought "wait, my team literally does all 5 of these manually".

DM me "AUTOMATE."

I'll look at your workflow and tell you exactly what to automate first.

NeoShield is recruiting a Research Fellow in Health Data Science with a focus on machine learning, based at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine, U. of London.

This is an opportunity for someone skilled in building statistical and machine learning systems who wants to see them implemented in real wards with real patients, whether their background is in academia, industry, or the public sector.

About the role:
The Research Fellow will have end-to-end technical ownership of two production ML systems within the NeoShield programme: a machine-learning-based Clinical Decision Support Tool for neonatal sepsis diagnosis, and a real-time ward-level outbreak detection and alert system. The post involves close collaboration with the Zambia National Public Health Institute (ZNPHI) and the Malawi Liverpool Wellcome Research Programme (MLW), with travel to both countries to support implementation within neonatal units.
This is a full-time position (Grade 6) on a 24-month contract with potential for extension, funded by the Wellcome Trust. The salary range is £45,728 to £51,872 inclusive of London weighting, and hybrid working is possible.

Experience with temporal or time-series data and data pipelines is important, as is strong version-controlled coding practice. The successful candidate will be comfortable working independently across a multidisciplinary, multi-country team.

Why this role matters:
Neonatal sepsis remains one of the leading causes of newborn mortality globally, concentrated in settings where diagnostics are limited and antibiotic decisions are made under pressure. NeoShield is linking laboratory diagnostics, clinical care, and machine learning to change that.

There's a seductive narrative making the rounds: one person, armed with AI, builds a billion-dollar company from their laptop.

Matthew Gallagher's MEDVi. $401M in year-one sales. On pace for $1.8B. Two employees. Built with $20K and AI tools in two months.

Matt is an impressive serial entrepreneur — the tribe I love and respect. What he pulled off is remarkable.

But this story is being twisted to prove something it wasn't intended to.

Matt's genius was seeing he didn't need to build the entire telehealth stack. He built a razor-sharp customer acquisition layer — and plugged into partners who brought the rest. CareValidate provides the doctors. OpenLoop handles the pharmacy. Contract engineers fix production. A media agency runs ads. Firms handle legal and finance.

That's not a one-person company. That's one brilliant founder orchestrating a network of companies — each full of people. The orchestration is the superpower. Let's not confuse it with doing it alone.

AI made Matt extraordinarily productive at what he controlled — code, website, ad creative, customer service bots. That's the genuine shift. But the headlines bury his founder grind:

His AI chatbot hallucinated drug prices. He ate the cost. A website change broke production — 200 customers lost while he sprinted home from a hike. As revenue grew, he replaced AI with humans in legal and compliance. He's even considering hiring — because he's lonely. We're still social animals!

Here's what I've learned building companies over almost 30 years:

"𝘽𝙪𝙞𝙡𝙙𝙞𝙣𝙜 𝙖 𝙥𝙧𝙤𝙙𝙪𝙘𝙩" 𝙖𝙣𝙙 "𝙗𝙪𝙞𝙡𝙙𝙞𝙣𝙜 𝙖 𝙘𝙤𝙢𝙥𝙖𝙣𝙮" 𝙖𝙧𝙚 𝙘𝙤𝙢𝙥𝙡𝙚𝙩𝙚𝙡𝙮 𝙙𝙞𝙛𝙛𝙚𝙧𝙚𝙣𝙩 𝙩𝙝𝙞𝙣𝙜𝙨.

A brilliant founder might be world-class at one thing. But durable companies require judgment across many domains at once: go-to-market, partnerships, capital allocation, customer trust, org design. That's not a prompt chain. That's a team.

Whatever clever AI workflow gives you an edge today gets absorbed by everyone else tomorrow. Capability commoditizes in weeks, not years. Your moat melts.

What doesn't commoditize? Proprietary knowledge. Customer relationships. Institutional judgment. Teams that compound their understanding over time.

That's what I see every day at Pinecone — 800K developers don't come back for our AI. They come back for the knowledge layer underneath it.

The real story isn't that one person replaces a company. It's that AI makes small, exceptional teams unreasonably powerful. A 3-person team today ships what took 30 five years ago. That's worth celebrating — not the myth of the lone genius.

AI is the most powerful leverage tool builders have ever had. Let's use it to build a sharper team — not to pretend you don't need one.

Navigating the 2026 Qatar Job Market: Why Soft Skills are the New Hard Currency 🇶🇦

The conversation around the Qatari job market is changing. We’ve moved past the "construction boom" era and entered a phase defined by Strategic Selectivity and Economic Diversification.

If you are looking for a role or looking to hire in Doha today, the flashy resume is no longer enough. The market is currently rewarding three specific traits:

1. Patience: The Long Game 
With the unemployment rate remaining among the lowest globally (approx. 0.1%), the market is tight. Employers are no longer "panic hiring" to meet tournament deadlines. They are taking their time to find the exact cultural and technical fit.  
• Advice: Don't be discouraged by longer recruitment cycles. Use this time to upskill in AI and digital literacy—the two biggest drivers in Qatar’s 2026 growth.

2. Stability: Beyond the Contract 
Qatar’s GDP is projected to grow by over 6% this year. This isn't just oil and gas; it’s healthcare, tech, and logistics. Companies are looking for "anchor" employees—people who want to build a career here, not just pass through.  
• Key sectors: Digital transformation, renewable energy, and specialized healthcare are the pillars of our current stability.

3. Reliability: The Trust Factor 
In a market that is increasingly skill-based rather than title-based, your reputation is your strongest asset. Being reliable in a hybrid work environment is now a top-tier soft skill. Employers in the Gulf are prioritizing candidates who can "grow with the company" rather than just fill a seat.  
The Bottom Line: The 2026 landscape is resilient but demanding. It requires a shift in mindset: from finding a job to providing a solution.

Whether you are an expat or a national, the opportunities are there—provided you bring the patience to find them and the reliability to keep them.

Success Metrics

That silence in the boardroom was deafening . The company was  bleeding money every month for seats, logins, and "AI features" that don't actually finish the job. For a founder scaling globally, paying for a software license when you haven't found your Country Head isn't just a cost—it’s a distraction.

If you are an Indian firm expanding into 90+ countries, you don't need another login to manage. You need an outcome. In the AI-native era, the software shouldn't just "assist" your team; it should orchestrate the delivery of the hire.

We believe that the next decade belongs to companies that take responsibility for the output. At CBREX , we don’t want to sell you a "seat" in our software. We want to fill the "seat" in your global office. We call this model: RAAR.

Just buying a recruiting  software is like buying a high-end treadmill and hoping you’ll get fit just by owning it. RAAR (Recruitment As A Result) is like hiring a personal trainer who only gets paid when you actually hit your target weight. One is a tool; the other is a Result.

The RAAR model of CBREX given you an AI-native talent supply chain that is built for one thing: The Hire.

- AI at the Heart: Our Hybrid RAG AI doesn't just "filter" resumes; it activates 4,500+ specialized global nodes to find the exact "Unicorn" you need.
- No License Fees: You don't pay for seats or subscriptions. You pay for the Result.
- Global Simplicity: One interface and one contract to source and hire across 90+ countries—with 100% focus on the outcome.

The era of paying for "shelf-ware" is over. The era of Recruitment As A Result is here.

The real question for your 2026 expansion roadmap is this: Are you still buying a license, or are you ready to buy a Result?

Now I show up to every client interaction the way I'm supposed to: focused on the work, not managing the infrastructure behind it.

If you're a remote freelancer or consultant still tolerating an unstable connection to save a few dollars a month calculate what one lost client actually costs you.

48 hours. 2 people. 1 idea that genuinely made us go — why doesn't this exist already?

Rudraksh Tiwari and i just wrapped up Kalpathon 2.0, a 2-day hackathon at BBD University, and we built something we're actually proud of — Lawffle ⚖️

The problem we kept thinking about: legal documents are a nightmare for anyone who isn't a lawyer. Students signing agreements they don't understand. Freelancers accepting contracts with sketchy clauses buried in legalese. Startups drafting NDAs from scratch because hiring a lawyer costs more than their monthly budget.

So we built Lawffle — an AI-powered legal document generator and simplifier.

Here's what it does:
→ Generate ready-to-use legal documents (NDAs, contracts, agreements) through a guided, step-by-step process
→ Paste any legal clause and it converts it into plain English instantly
→ Automatically detects and highlights risky clauses before you sign anything
→ Download professionally formatted PDFs, ready to use

Under the hood: Next.js + TypeScript on the frontend, Node.js + Express on the backend, Claude Sonnet API (Anthropic) doing the AI heavy lifting, deployed on Vercel and Render.

We didn't win. But we shipped something real, learned an insane amount in 48 hours, and honestly — watching Lawffle actually simplify a dense legal clause into two readable sentences felt like enough of a win for me.

It's live. It's on GitHub. Go break it (or don't).

How I use AI and Automation #9 - Reminders and follow-up

Today's song quote: Simple Minds - Don't you (forget about me)
"Don't you forget about me.
Don't, don't, don't, don't"
https://lnkd.in/dawhwNit

Follow-ups and reminders are often the most time-consuming tasks in HR processes. In hiring processes, for example, all companies require information from new employees when onboarding, and everyone needs to sign contracts.

It sounds simple when it comes to single hires. For bulk hiring however, it quickly becomes a burden and tasks can easily fall through the cracks.

So there is huge value in automating these tasks.

At Icelandair we've been working specifically on bulk hiring and onboarding processes recently, and we're continuously improving the processes as we go. A few things have stood out as being the most obvious time-saving processes.


- Structured Forms. What seems obvious to you, might not be so obvious to the next person. We've hit issues here repeatedly, and we've had to reiterate wording and structure of our forms multiple times. In more complex cases you can of course also use AI to review data or attachment from the form to verify.

- Automatic Document Creation of contracts, applications and other documents. We've saved so much time here. Instead of generating contracts and sending one by one, we now create all of them automatically and send them automatically for electronic signing.

- Automatic Reminders. This can apply for every action that you require from the new employee where you can verify completion of the task. So we can set this up for both forms and contract signing. By automating reminders we get rid of all that manual follow-up, and we make sure that we don't, don't don't, don't forget about you.
